author: @justinw1765
text:
Depending on how hot it gets, won't some binders/glues start to degrade?  If you chucked a bunch of graphenic graphite and/or copper powder into say sodium silicate ("waterglass"), would that work?  It would definitely add a lot of resistivity to it, but maybe too much? 

   I could see a specialized Stirling engine for a small, lightweight, aerodynamic vehicle (like the Aptera) that works from Solar heating during the day and then at night works off batteries. By specialization, I mean optimizing the heat and cold differentials.  Like use vacuum insulation over the part to get hot plus with ultra black carbon nanotubes to absorb light, and then extremely thermally conductive materials* + evap system for the part to cool.  The heater part would be placed in a Solar "funnel cooker" type design concentrator as it doesn't need to be adjusted for Solar position near as much. 

* I wonder how graphinenic graphite in copper would do?  I've lately been thinking about building an insulated thermite foundry. The idea is to take one of those large (64 oz) vacuum insulated stainless steel containers (put that in a large clay flower pot), put some ceramic fiber insulation and sand on the bottom of the insulated container, put a large high heat crucible in the middle, pack the sides with ceramic fiber and sand (on both the bottom and sides, the ceramic fiber insulation would be directly against the SS vacuum insulated container).  Then put some thermite in the crucible.  Let it react for a bit, throw in your copper, and then as it's melting throw in your graphenic graphite, and take an equal sized (64 oz or whatever) vacuum insulated container and put over the top but with leaving an air gap so it doesn't pressurize and fly off.  Or something more safe would be to take carbon fire/welding blanket or ceramic fiber insulation and put it over the top to insulate it (you would only do this after the reaction has died down some because otherwise it will burn through most likely).

   The idea is that the ceramic fiber insulation and sand provides some protection for the SS insulated container so it doesn't melt/warp, the sand provides thermal mass to keep it warmer for longer, the vacuum insulation keeps it warmer for longer, and the ceramic fiber insulation top also helps to keep it warmer for longer.  Theoretically, you should be able to fully melt the copper and be able to mix in the graphenic graphite in with it.  I would try with equal volumes of the copper to graphenic graphite.

     I also plan to use it to convert carbon sources into graphite using the above, by putting the carbon source on the bottom of the crucible, putting multiple sheets of paper over that, putting some activated carbon on top of that, and then putting the thermite reaction materials on top of the carbon. Because it reaches such a high temp, it should produce some nice graphites, especially if you use a source that contains a high percentage of crystalline cellulose.  

 Wonder how long the crucible would last?

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: Actually, I don't think the sand would work. The sand closest to the crucible would likely melt into glass and then taking out the crucible and/or reusing it would be a bit of a pane...   I think activated carbon (or any carbon) around the crucible would work better. And while it won't provide as much thermal mass as the sand, it will help to insulate the SS vacuum insulated container better.  Actually might also use NightHawkinLight's approximate Starlyte material to directly line the SS container as well, then a layer of ceramic fiber insulation, then the activated carbon to pack in the crucible.  Actually, that combo would protect the SS container far better, and provide more insulation for the crucible.

author: @stefm8251
text:
i had an idea wich is close to that ! but instead of using a stirling engine my idea is to make an engine with Nitinol spring that will replace piston , connected to a crankshaft like normal engine and using electricity to heat up water/graphene mixed with ultrasonic transducer and resonating plate kind like kelly device very efficient to heat water with very litlle input and spray that hot water or steam on the Nitinol spring with a central 360" injector , the engine will not run on compression but expansion and contraction of a memory form alloy and Nitinol can generate an incredible strenght when changing from hot to cold state or vice versa wich could lead to a torquy and light weight engine , this will probably not be a high RPM motor but thats also an advantage if you dont need a gear box to slow down the rotational speed of the motor to match the one needed at the wheel . the all thing powered by a graphene hemp based supercapacitor battery .
not sure if its reel but i saw a video of a device using a 9volt alkaline battery using resonator and ultrasound to heat up to boiling temp 1,7 gallon of water in under 2 minute starting from room temp, if its true that could be the way to go to make electric car affordable , efficient , long range and diminish drastically the pollution generated during the production of electric car.
for what i know about Nitinol its made of 60/40 titanium/nikel and depending of the thickness it require between 40'c to 80'c to change shape ,easily achievable with hot waterand graphene in it will increase the heat transfer.Nitinol  can be buy raw wit no memory form programmed or already shaped but is easy to program the shape using heat .
you can find paperclip made out of nitinol to recognize them change their shape and put them in hot water if they change back to their original form they are made of Nitinol and use them in a model engine like your stirling one, it can be cool!

author: @aomanchutube
text: You know in the north here in the USA, I've seen floor installatios that include pex water heating. Basically hot water flows from the boiler to the floor. But that means you can't just nail new flooring. How do you know where the pex is?. But with conductive ink or conductive sand or cement, that would not be a problem at all. For example, the product could be an ink or glue that you would use to bind regular play sand under the flooring. This would work as self leveling compound and at the same time, it would be the floor heating system.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's an awesome idea mate

author: @scotttovey
text:
OK!
Now hook up a small generator to it and see how much current you can produce.

Could you use it to keep water flowing down a SWAG? - I'd be careful with this one cause a long enough stretch and you could end up breaking the universe by generating more electricity than you use.

Now that it comes to mind; have you thought of testing to see if your conductive ink could work with a magneto-hydrodynamic salt water propulsion system?
 
Given that there are no moving parts, would a magneto-hydrodynamic salt water propulsion system be more efficient than a standard water pump?

replies.author: @brothernobody1775

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: Over unity will not happen but if you can take advantage of supplemental naturally occurring inputs it will look like you have and it leads many people astray when trying to repeat the experiments without them because they did not know they were there to begin with.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text: @Barskor1 😎👍

replies.author: @RWBHere
replies.text: The important question to ask is, 'How much power can it produce, compared to the input power?' The answer will likely be less than 25%. It will be a good learning experience, and can teach you ways to reduce inefficiencies in a system.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@RWBHere 
If you want to learn how to reduce inefficiencies; follow what the inventor of the Oxford Electric Bell did.
https://youtu.be/UtQGYz4f3YQ

I did the a bit of searching so I could do the calculation.
At 2 kilo-volts and 1 nanoamphere, the battery puts out 2 micro-watts of energy every time the bells chime and has been doing that for 170+ years.

I'm not sure which is the more remarkable, the battery lasting for over 170 years, or the movement actuating on 2 micro-watts of energy.

[EDIT]
After re-watching the video and paying a bit closer attention, Sally Le Page mentions that the bell was made by London instrument makers Watkin and Hill in 1825. It's 194 years old and will be 200 in 2025.

text:
Great stuff Robert, as always! :) I really can’t wait to see commercial products based on your ink, there’s so many applications for it, besides batteries. 
At the moment I think of printing a spiral on a paper tube, rolling it in a flat bagel shape to make a motor coil. That would be awesome to see the ink move things. Another idea I have is to print it in a way to be a coil and a power storage device at the same time. What do you think about that?

Happy holidays, mate!
